purpose: Link Layer Discovery Protocol for neighbor and topology discovery -- advertises device identity, capabilities, and port information to directly connected neighbors over Ethernet

states.name: DISABLED
states.description: LLDP is administratively disabled on the port. The port will not transmit or process LLDP frames. In SONiC this state is entered when lldp_enabled is set to false in CONFIG_DB PORT table or when the port is admin-down. lldpd does not register the interface for LLDP operation.
verify_commands: docker exec lldp lldpcli show neighbors

states.name: INIT
states.description: LLDP port state machine has been initialized. The reinit-delay timer is running before the first LLDP frame is transmitted. This prevents rapid LLDP frame bursts when a port comes up. The port listens for incoming LLDP frames during this window. In SONiC, lldpmgrd triggers this transition when it detects a port-up event from CONFIG_DB.
verify_commands: docker exec lldp lldpcli show neighbors
docker exec lldp supervisorctl status lldpd

states.name: TX_WAIT
states.description: The reinit-delay has expired and lldpd is waiting for the tx-interval timer before sending the first LLDP frame. The port is actively listening for LLDP frames from the neighbor. This is a brief transient state before the first transmission.
verify_commands: docker exec lldp lldpcli show neighbors

states.name: TX
states.description: lldpd is transmitting LLDP frames at the configured tx-interval. The port is sending LLDPDUs containing the local device's TLVs to the multicast address 01:80:C2:00:00:0E. Frames are also being received and processed from the neighbor. This is the normal operating state for an LLDP-enabled port with a connected neighbor.
verify_commands: docker exec lldp lldpcli show neighbors

states.name: NEIGHBOR_PRESENT
states.description: At least one valid LLDP neighbor has been discovered on the port. The neighbor entry is cached by lldpd with a TTL equal to tx-interval * tx-hold-multiplier. lldpsyncd has published the neighbor information to STATE_DB LLDP_ENTRY table. This is the fully operational steady state for a connected LLDP port.
verify_commands: docker exec lldp lldpcli show neighbors

states.name: NEIGHBOR_AGED_OUT
states.description: A previously discovered LLDP neighbor entry has exceeded its TTL (tx-interval * tx-hold-multiplier) without being refreshed. lldpd removes the neighbor from its cache and lldpsyncd deletes the STATE_DB LLDP_ENTRY entry. This can indicate a link failure, a neighbor reboot, or the neighbor has disabled LLDP.
verify_commands: docker exec lldp lldpcli show neighbors

transitions.trigger: Port admin-up event received; lldpmgrd sets lldp_enabled in CONFIG_DB; lldpd registers the interface for LLDP operation and starts reinit-delay timer
reinit-delay timer expires (default 2s); port is now eligible to transmit LLDP frames; tx-interval timer started
transitions.failure_if_stuck: lldpd not running, or port removed from lldpd interface list before reinit completed

transitions.trigger: tx-interval timer fires; lldpd constructs LLDPDU from local TLVs and sends to 01:80:C2:00:00:0E multicast; continues transmitting at tx-interval
transitions.failure_if_stuck: CoPP rate-limiting LLDP egress, or lldpd cannot bind to the interface (e.g., interface in LAG and LLDP not enabled on LAG member)

transitions.trigger: Valid LLDPDU received from neighbor; lldpd parses mandatory TLVs (chassis ID, port ID, TTL) and stores neighbor entry; lldpsyncd publishes to STATE_DB
transitions.failure_if_stuck: CoPP dropping incoming LLDP frames, neighbor not sending LLDP, or lldpsyncd not running

transitions.trigger: Neighbor TTL expires without receiving a refresh LLDPDU (ttl = tx-interval * tx-hold-multiplier); lldpd ages out the entry and notifies lldpsyncd
Port is still physically up and lldpd continues transmitting; waiting for neighbor to send a new LLDPDU to repopulate the entry
transitions.failure_if_stuck: Neighbor disabled LLDP, cable replaced with non-LLDP device, or CoPP rate-limiting inbound LLDP frames

transitions.trigger: Port admin-down or lldp_enabled set to false; lldpd sends LLDPDU with TTL=0 (shutdown TLV) to notify neighbor, then removes port from operation
Port admin-down event; lldpmgrd detects port state change and instructs lldpd to stop LLDP on that interface

timers.name: tx-interval
timers.description: Interval between consecutive LLDP frame transmissions on a port. Determines how frequently the local device advertises its presence and TLV information to neighbors. Also controls the base value for TTL calculation (TTL = tx-interval * tx-hold-multiplier).
timers.on_expiry: lldpd transmits a new LLDPDU on all active LLDP-enabled ports
timers.sonic_config_path: CONFIG_DB DEVICE_METADATA|localhost: lldp_tx_interval field; also configurable via 'config lldp interval <seconds>'
timers.sonic_frr_path: N/A -- LLDP is not managed by FRR in SONiC
timers.tuning_advice: Lower for faster topology discovery in dynamic environments (e.g., 10s). Increasing reduces CPU and bandwidth overhead on large port-count switches. TTL automatically adjusts with this value.

timers.name: tx-hold-multiplier
timers.description: Multiplied by tx-interval to compute the TTL advertised in LLDP frames. Neighbors age out the local device entry after TTL seconds without a refresh. Higher values mean neighbors hold stale entries longer after a failure.
timers.on_expiry: N/A -- this is a multiplier, not a countdown timer; TTL = tx-interval * tx-hold is encoded in each LLDPDU
timers.sonic_config_path: CONFIG_DB DEVICE_METADATA|localhost: lldp_tx_hold field; also configurable via 'config lldp hold <multiplier>'
timers.tuning_advice: Increase to 6-8 if LLDP neighbors flap during normal traffic spikes (CoPP drops causing missed refreshes). Decrease to 2-3 for faster stale-entry cleanup in environments with frequent topology changes.

timers.name: reinit-delay
timers.description: Minimum time to wait before transmitting the first LLDP frame after a port comes up or LLDP is re-enabled. Prevents LLDP frame bursts when many ports come up simultaneously (e.g., after a switch reboot).
timers.on_expiry: Port transitions from INIT to TX_WAIT and may begin transmitting LLDPDUs
timers.sonic_config_path: Not directly exposed in CONFIG_DB; controlled by lldpd configuration generated by lldpmgrd
timers.tuning_advice: Generally leave at default. Only lower if fast LLDP neighbor discovery is required immediately after port-up events (e.g., automated cabling verification workflows).

messages.name: LLDPDU (Link Layer Discovery Protocol Data Unit)
messages.description: The sole message type in LLDP. Sent as an Ethernet frame to the well-known multicast address 01:80:C2:00:00:0E with EtherType 0x88CC. Contains an ordered sequence of TLVs (Type-Length-Value) starting with three mandatory TLVs and ending with End-of-LLDPDU TLV. Each device transmits LLDPDUs at tx-interval and processes received LLDPDUs to build its neighbor table.
messages.when_sent: Periodically at tx-interval on every LLDP-enabled port; also sent immediately with TTL=0 (shutdown TLV sequence) when LLDP is administratively disabled on a port
key_fields: Chassis ID TLV (Type 1, mandatory): uniquely identifies the transmitting device -- in SONiC typically the MAC address of the management port or a configured system identifier
Port ID TLV (Type 2, mandatory): identifies the specific port -- in SONiC typically the interface name (e.g., Ethernet0) or port MAC address
Time To Live TLV (Type 3, mandatory): TTL in seconds = tx-interval * tx-hold-multiplier; value 0 signals administrative shutdown
Port Description TLV (Type 4, optional): human-readable port description -- maps to interface description in SONiC CONFIG_DB
System Name TLV (Type 5, optional): device hostname -- taken from CONFIG_DB DEVICE_METADATA|localhost hostname field
System Description TLV (Type 6, optional): free-form text about the device -- SONiC includes OS version and platform info
System Capabilities TLV (Type 7, optional): bitmask of device capabilities (bridge, router, etc.) and which are enabled
Management Address TLV (Type 8, optional): management IP address(es) and interface index -- used for SNMP/NMS correlation
IEEE 802.1 OUI Extension TLVs: VLAN Name, Port VLAN ID, Link Aggregation (LAG membership)
IEEE 802.3 OUI Extension TLVs: MAC/PHY Configuration, Power via MDI, Link Aggregation, Maximum Frame Size
End-of-LLDPDU TLV (Type 0, mandatory): zero-length TLV marking end of the LLDPDU

failure_modes.scenario: No LLDP neighbors discovered on any port
failure_modes.description: The switch is running but 'show lldp table' or 'show lldp neighbors' returns no entries. lldpd may not be running, all ports may be admin-down, CoPP may be dropping all inbound LLDP frames, or lldpsyncd may not be publishing neighbor data to STATE_DB. This is the most common LLDP failure and is often discovered when automated topology validation scripts return empty neighbor tables.
symptoms: 'show lldp table' returns no entries or an empty table
'docker exec lldp lldpcli show neighbors' returns 'No neighbor found'
redis-cli -n 6 KEYS 'LLDP_ENTRY_TABLE|*' returns empty
Neighbor switches show LLDP entries for their side of the link but this switch shows nothing
lldpd process shows as stopped or repeatedly restarting in 'docker exec lldp supervisorctl status'
root_causes: lldpd process not running inside the lldp container -- crash or supervisord not starting it
lldp Docker container itself is not running or is in a restart loop
All ports are admin-down -- lldpd does not transmit or receive on admin-down interfaces
CoPP (Control Plane Policing) rate-limiting or dropping LLDP frames destined for the CPU -- lldpd never sees incoming frames
lldpsyncd not running -- lldpd has neighbors in its cache but they are never published to STATE_DB
lldpmgrd not running -- lldpd has not been given the interface list from CONFIG_DB and is operating on no interfaces
Network device between switches filtering EtherType 0x88CC frames (some intermediate devices do not forward LLDP multicast)
resolution: Check lldp container: 'docker ps | grep lldp' -- should show Up status
Check all lldp processes: 'docker exec lldp supervisorctl status' -- lldpd, lldpsyncd, and lldpmgrd should all be RUNNING
Check port admin status: 'show interfaces status' -- verify ports are admin up
Check CoPP: 'show policer' or 'redis-cli -n 0 HGETALL "COPP_TABLE:trap.group.lldp"' for LLDP trap configuration
Capture LLDP frames on the wire: 'sudo tcpdump -i <ifname> ether proto 0x88cc -c 5' -- if frames appear here but not in lldp table, suspect CoPP or lldpsyncd
Check STATE_DB: 'redis-cli -n 6 KEYS "LLDP_ENTRY_TABLE|*"' -- if lldpd has neighbors but STATE_DB is empty, lldpsyncd is the issue
Restart lldp container if all processes appear stopped: 'sudo systemctl restart lldp'

verify_commands.look_for: lldp container status -- should show 'Up' not 'Exited' or 'Restarting'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Exited or Restarting status confirms lldp container is not running -- root cause of all LLDP failures

verify_commands.look_for: lldpd, lldpsyncd, and lldpmgrd should all show RUNNING state
verify_commands.confirms_if: Any process in STOPPED or FATAL state confirms that specific component is the failure point

verify_commands.command: docker exec lldp lldpcli show neighbors
verify_commands.look_for: 'No neighbor found' vs actual neighbor entries -- distinguishes whether lldpd itself has no neighbors or whether lldpsyncd is failing to publish
verify_commands.confirms_if: If lldpcli shows neighbors but 'show lldp table' is empty, lldpsyncd is the problem; if lldpcli also shows nothing, suspect CoPP or port state

verify_commands.look_for: LLDP frames arriving on the wire from the neighbor
verify_commands.confirms_if: Frames seen by tcpdump but not appearing in lldp table confirms CoPP is dropping them before lldpd can process them

verify_commands.look_for: List of interface keys with LLDP neighbor data in STATE_DB
verify_commands.confirms_if: Empty STATE_DB when lldpd has neighbors confirms lldpsyncd is not publishing; populated STATE_DB rules out lldpsyncd as the issue

verify_commands.look_for: Admin state column -- ports must be 'up' for LLDP to operate
verify_commands.confirms_if: All ports showing admin-down explains why no LLDP neighbors are discovered

failure_modes.scenario: Stale LLDP neighbor entries -- old neighbors persist after cable swap
failure_modes.description: After replacing a cable or moving a connection to a different neighbor, the old LLDP neighbor entry remains in the neighbor table instead of being replaced by the new neighbor. This causes topology confusion in NMS systems and can delay automated topology-validation workflows. The stale entry should age out after TTL (tx-interval * tx-hold-multiplier) seconds but this can take 2 minutes or more with default settings.
symptoms: 'show lldp neighbors <ifname>' shows the old neighbor's system name and chassis ID after a cable swap
NMS or topology discovery tools report incorrect neighbor relationships
New cable is physically connected and new neighbor is sending LLDP frames, but old entry is still shown
STATE_DB LLDP_ENTRY_TABLE still contains the old neighbor's data
'docker exec lldp lldpcli show neighbors' also shows the stale entry (not just a display/sync issue)
root_causes: LLDP TTL not yet expired -- default TTL is 120s (30s * 4); stale entry is expected until TTL countdown completes
Old neighbor sent a shutdown LLDPDU (TTL=0) but it was not received or was dropped by CoPP before lldpd could process it
lldpsyncd crashed and restarted, leaving a stale STATE_DB entry that was not cleaned up during the restart
lldpd process was restarted without flushing its neighbor cache, leaving orphan STATE_DB entries
New neighbor on the port is not sending LLDP frames yet (or has LLDP disabled), so the old entry simply ages out on TTL without being replaced
resolution: Wait for natural TTL expiry (up to 120s with defaults) -- this is normal behavior, not a bug
Force immediate flush: 'docker exec lldp lldpcli update ports' to trigger an immediate re-scan
Clear specific neighbor manually: 'docker exec lldp lldpcli pause' then 'lldpcli resume' to force a resync
Check if the new neighbor is actually sending LLDP: 'sudo tcpdump -i <ifname> ether proto 0x88cc -c 5'
If lldpsyncd left orphan entries: 'redis-cli -n 6 DEL "LLDP_ENTRY_TABLE|<ifname>"' (use with caution -- lldpsyncd will repopulate on next update)
Reduce tx-hold-multiplier to 2 if faster stale-entry cleanup is needed in high-churn environments: 'config lldp hold 2'

verify_commands.command: docker exec lldp lldpcli show neighbors
verify_commands.look_for: age field on each neighbor entry -- shows how many seconds since last LLDPDU was received; high age indicates the neighbor stopped sending
verify_commands.confirms_if: Age close to TTL value confirms this is a normal TTL-expiry scenario, not a lldpsyncd bug

verify_commands.look_for: lldp_rem_sys_name, lldp_rem_port_id fields -- compare against expected new neighbor values
verify_commands.confirms_if: Old system name still in STATE_DB confirms stale entry has not been replaced or aged out yet

verify_commands.look_for: Source MAC and system name in LLDP frames -- should show new neighbor after cable swap
verify_commands.confirms_if: New neighbor's MAC in frames but old entry in table confirms lldpd is receiving but not yet replacing the cached entry (within TTL window)

verify_commands.command: docker exec lldp lldpcli show statistics
verify_commands.look_for: Frame discard and frame error counts per port -- elevated discards suggest CoPP dropping shutdown LLDPDUs
verify_commands.confirms_if: High frame discard count on the affected port suggests CoPP may have dropped the old neighbor's shutdown LLDPDU

failure_modes.scenario: Missing or incorrect TLV information in LLDP neighbor entries
failure_modes.description: LLDP neighbors are discovered and appear in 'show lldp table' but key TLV fields are wrong, empty, or missing: system name shows an IP address instead of hostname, port description is blank, or management address is not populated. This typically indicates a configuration issue on the local switch that prevents lldpd from correctly populating its own TLVs before advertising them.
symptoms: 'show lldp neighbors' shows neighbors but system name is blank or shows a raw MAC/IP instead of hostname
Port description field is empty in neighbor LLDP advertisements from this switch
Management address TLV is missing or shows the wrong IP address
Remote switches report incorrect system information for this SONiC device
NMS systems show wrong device identity despite LLDP being operational
root_causes: Hostname not configured in CONFIG_DB DEVICE_METADATA|localhost -- lldpd uses this for System Name TLV; defaults to a generic identifier if not set
Port description not set in CONFIG_DB PORT|<ifname> description field -- lldpd will advertise a blank or auto-generated port description
Management address not configured or lldpmgrd has not pushed the correct management IP to lldpd
lldpmgrd crashed or has not run since boot -- lldpd is running with stale or default TLV values not reflecting current CONFIG_DB state
lldp container was started before CONFIG_DB was fully populated (race condition at boot) -- lldpmgrd read incomplete configuration
Chassis ID type mismatch -- lldpd configured to use MAC address but NMS expects interface name or vice versa
resolution: Verify hostname: 'redis-cli -n 4 HGETALL "DEVICE_METADATA|localhost"' -- check hostname field
Set hostname if missing: 'sudo config hostname <name>' then verify lldpmgrd picks it up
Check port descriptions: 'redis-cli -n 4 HGETALL "PORT|<ifname>"' -- check description field
Restart lldpmgrd to force re-read of CONFIG_DB: 'docker exec lldp supervisorctl restart lldpmgrd'
Verify lldpd TLV state: 'docker exec lldp lldpcli show configuration' -- shows what lldpd has been told to advertise
Check lldpmgrd logs: 'docker exec lldp cat /var/log/lldpmgrd.log' or 'docker logs lldp 2>&1 | grep lldpmgrd'

verify_commands.look_for: hostname field -- must be set for LLDP System Name TLV to advertise the correct hostname
verify_commands.confirms_if: Empty or missing hostname field explains why neighbor switches see a generic identifier instead of the device hostname

verify_commands.command: docker exec lldp lldpcli show configuration
verify_commands.look_for: System description, hostname, capabilities, and management address lldpd has been configured to advertise
verify_commands.confirms_if: Wrong or missing values here confirm lldpmgrd has not correctly pushed CONFIG_DB state to lldpd

verify_commands.command: docker exec lldp supervisorctl status lldpmgrd
verify_commands.look_for: lldpmgrd RUNNING state and uptime -- recently restarted lldpmgrd may not have completed its CONFIG_DB sync
verify_commands.confirms_if: STOPPED or very recently restarted lldpmgrd explains why TLV values have not been updated

verify_commands.look_for: Raw LLDPDU content -- hex dump shows exact TLV values being transmitted; compare system name TLV to expected hostname
verify_commands.confirms_if: LLDPDU in wire capture showing wrong system name confirms lldpd is advertising incorrect local TLVs

verify_commands.command: docker logs lldp 2>&1 | grep -i lldpmgrd | tail -30
verify_commands.look_for: CONFIG_DB subscription errors, template rendering failures, or lldpcli invocation failures in lldpmgrd output
verify_commands.confirms_if: Error messages in lldpmgrd log confirm it failed to push correct TLV configuration to lldpd

failure_modes.scenario: lldpd process crash or excessive memory consumption
failure_modes.description: The lldpd process inside the lldp container crashes repeatedly or consumes abnormally high memory, causing the container to be OOM-killed or lldpd to be continuously restarted by supervisord. This results in LLDP neighbor tables being intermittently empty or oscillating. lldpd is vulnerable to malformed LLDP frames from misbehaving neighbors and can also accumulate memory on very high port-count switches with many neighbors.
symptoms: lldp neighbor table intermittently empty then repopulates -- oscillating LLDP state
'docker exec lldp supervisorctl status lldpd' shows lldpd STARTING or with frequent restart timestamps
'docker inspect lldp | grep RestartCount' shows non-zero restart count
'docker stats lldp --no-stream' shows abnormally high memory usage for the lldp container
syslog shows OOM kill events for the lldp container or lldpd process
journalctl or syslog shows core dump or segfault from lldpd
root_causes: Malformed LLDP frame from a neighbor triggering a parsing bug in lldpd -- some third-party NICs or embedded devices send non-compliant LLDPDUs
Excessive number of LLDP neighbors -- switches with 128+ ports and 2 neighbors per port can accumulate significant neighbor state in lldpd memory
lldpd receiving LLDP frames with abnormally large TLV values or excessively long string fields that trigger buffer issues
lldpd bug in the SONiC-bundled version -- check if a known lldpd CVE or bug applies to the running SONiC version
Memory leak in lldpd accumulating over long uptime -- may not be reproducible in short-term testing
lldp container hitting Docker cgroup memory limit -- the limit may be set too low for high port-count platforms
resolution: Check lldpd crash frequency: 'docker inspect lldp | grep RestartCount'
Check for OOM: 'sudo dmesg | grep -i oom | grep -i lldp'
Check memory: 'docker stats lldp --no-stream'
Identify offending port: 'docker exec lldp lldpcli show statistics' -- look for frame error spikes on specific ports
Temporarily disable LLDP rx on the offending port: 'docker exec lldp lldpcli configure ports <ifname> lldp status rx-only' then 'tx-only' then 'disabled' to isolate
Capture the malformed frame: 'sudo tcpdump -i <ifname> ether proto 0x88cc -c 10 -w /tmp/lldp_debug.pcap'
Update SONiC to a version with a newer lldpd that addresses known parsing issues
If memory leak: schedule periodic lldpd restart via cron as a workaround while investigating root cause

verify_commands.look_for: RestartCount > 0 indicates lldpd container has been restarted
verify_commands.confirms_if: Non-zero RestartCount confirms lldpd is crashing and being restarted by Docker

verify_commands.look_for: MEM USAGE / LIMIT -- unusually high memory for an LLDP daemon (normal is <50MB; concern at >200MB)
verify_commands.confirms_if: Memory at or near container limit confirms OOM pressure is the cause of lldpd restarts

verify_commands.command: sudo dmesg | grep -i oom | grep -i lldp | tail -10
verify_commands.look_for: OOM kill events targeting lldpd or the lldp container cgroup
verify_commands.confirms_if: OOM kill event for lldpd confirms memory exhaustion is causing the crash-restart cycle

verify_commands.command: docker exec lldp lldpcli show statistics
verify_commands.look_for: Frame discards, frame errors, and TLV discards per port -- spikes on a specific port indicate a misbehaving neighbor
verify_commands.confirms_if: Elevated frame errors on one specific port identifies the likely source of the malformed frames crashing lldpd

verify_commands.look_for: Crash signals, segfault, or SIGKILL events in lldpd output
verify_commands.confirms_if: Segfault or signal 11 in lldpd logs confirms a parsing/memory crash rather than a graceful shutdown

failure_modes.scenario: CoPP rate-limiting dropping LLDP frames on high port-count switches
failure_modes.description: On switches with 48 or more ports, the volume of inbound LLDP frames (one per port every tx-interval seconds) can exceed the CoPP (Control Plane Policing) rate limit for the LLDP traffic class. When the CoPP policer drops frames, lldpd misses LLDP refreshes from neighbors, causing neighbor TTLs to expire prematurely and entries to age out even though the link and neighbor are physically healthy. This is a SONiC-specific operational hazard most visible on 64-port or 128-port spine switches.
symptoms: LLDP neighbors intermittently disappear from 'show lldp table' on high port-count switches despite links being up
'show lldp table' shows neighbors with very recent discovery but then they vanish within 1-2 refresh cycles
CoPP counters show drops in the LLDP traffic class: 'redis-cli -n 0 HGETALL "COPP_TABLE:trap.group.lldp"'
Problem is worse during periods of high control-plane load (BGP convergence, STP topology change, etc.)
'sudo tcpdump -i <ifname> ether proto 0x88cc' shows frames arriving at the NIC but lldpd statistics show fewer received frames than expected
Reducing tx-interval temporarily resolves the issue (more frequent frames means fewer consecutive drops needed to age out an entry)
root_causes: LLDP CoPP policer rate too low for the port count -- default is typically 200-500 pps which is sufficient for <32 ports at 30s interval but not for 64+ ports
LLDP and other L2 control traffic (LACP, STP BPDUs) sharing the same CoPP queue, exhausting the shared budget
CPU already under heavy load from BGP convergence, orchagent processing, or other control-plane work -- LLDP packets queued in the trap buffer but not processed in time
Platform-specific ASIC CoPP default is too conservative for SONiC high port-count deployments
CoPP configuration drift -- COPP_TABLE in APPL_DB does not match what coppmgrd intended due to a config reload issue
resolution: Check CoPP counters: 'show policer' -- look for drops in the lldp or l2 control class
Check APPL_DB CoPP config: 'redis-cli -n 0 HGETALL "COPP_TABLE:trap.group.lldp"'
Increase CoPP rate for LLDP: edit /etc/sonic/copp_cfg.json and increase cir/cbs for the LLDP trap group, then reload CoPP config
Increase tx-hold-multiplier as a workaround: 'config lldp hold 6' -- requires 6 consecutive missed refreshes to age out instead of 4
Increase tx-interval to reduce total LLDP frame rate: 'config lldp interval 60' -- halves inbound frame rate on a 64-port switch
Check if LLDP shares a CoPP group with other protocols and separate them if platform supports it
After any CoPP change: 'config reload' or 'sudo systemctl restart swss' to apply

verify_commands.look_for: Drop counts in LLDP or L2 control policer entries -- any drops indicate rate-limiting is occurring
verify_commands.confirms_if: Non-zero drop count in the LLDP CoPP class confirms frames are being policed before reaching lldpd

verify_commands.look_for: cir (committed information rate) and cbs (committed burst size) values -- compare against port count * (1/tx-interval) pps
verify_commands.confirms_if: CIR lower than total expected LLDP frame rate confirms CoPP is the bottleneck

verify_commands.command: docker exec lldp lldpcli show statistics
verify_commands.look_for: Frames received count per port -- if count is lower than tx-interval would predict, frames are being dropped before lldpd
verify_commands.confirms_if: Received frame count significantly lower than transmitted by neighbor confirms CoPP or kernel drop before lldpd

verify_commands.look_for: SAI_POLICER_STAT_GREEN_PACKETS vs SAI_POLICER_STAT_RED_PACKETS ratio -- red packets are dropped by CoPP
verify_commands.confirms_if: High red packet count in ASIC CoPP counters confirms ASIC-level LLDP frame dropping

verify_commands.look_for: Frame arrival rate -- if neighbor is sending at expected interval but lldpd statistics show fewer arrivals, CoPP is dropping between NIC and CPU
verify_commands.confirms_if: Frames visible to tcpdump (NIC) but not counted in lldpd statistics confirms kernel or CoPP drop in the path from NIC to lldpd socket

dependencies.name: lldpd
dependencies.description: The core LLDP protocol daemon inside the lldp Docker container. Handles LLDP frame transmission and reception, maintains the neighbor table, and advertises local TLVs. lldpd is an open-source implementation (lldpd.github.io) bundled with SONiC. All LLDP protocol state lives here.

dependencies.name: lldpmgrd
dependencies.description: SONiC management daemon that bridges CONFIG_DB and lldpd. Subscribes to CONFIG_DB changes (port descriptions, hostname, management addresses) and calls lldpcli to update lldpd's advertised TLVs. Without lldpmgrd, lldpd operates with stale or default TLV values that do not reflect the current SONiC configuration.

dependencies.name: lldpsyncd
dependencies.description: SONiC synchronization daemon that reads lldpd's neighbor table (via lldpcli) and publishes it to STATE_DB LLDP_ENTRY_TABLE. Without lldpsyncd, LLDP neighbor data exists in lldpd's internal state but is never visible to 'show lldp neighbors', NMS systems, or other SONiC components that consume STATE_DB.

dependencies.name: orchagent (swss container)
dependencies.description: Consumes STATE_DB LLDP_ENTRY_TABLE for neighbor correlation and topology awareness. LLDP neighbor data can be used by orchagent for port identification and by the SONiC management framework for topology queries. Not required for basic LLDP operation but needed for LLDP data to feed higher-level SONiC features.

dependencies.name: CoPP (Control Plane Policing)
dependencies.description: ASIC-level rate limiter for control-plane traffic. LLDP frames are trapped from the ASIC forwarding pipeline to the CPU via a dedicated CoPP trap entry. If the LLDP CoPP policer is too restrictive (low CIR/CBS) for the port count, frames are dropped before lldpd can receive them, causing neighbor entries to age out prematurely.

dependencies.name: CONFIG_DB (Redis DB 4)
dependencies.description: Source of truth for LLDP configuration: DEVICE_METADATA|localhost (hostname, lldp_tx_interval, lldp_tx_hold), PORT|<ifname> (description, admin_status). lldpmgrd reads CONFIG_DB to populate lldpd's local TLV advertisements.

dependencies.name: STATE_DB (Redis DB 6)
dependencies.description: Destination for discovered LLDP neighbor data. lldpsyncd writes LLDP_ENTRY_TABLE entries keyed by interface name. The 'show lldp neighbors' command reads from STATE_DB. External NMS and automation tools query STATE_DB to get neighbor topology data.

dependencies.name: APPL_DB (Redis DB 0)
dependencies.description: Contains the COPP_TABLE configuration for LLDP trap groups used by coppmgrd and orchagent to program ASIC CoPP rules. The LLDP CoPP policer rate directly affects whether LLDP frames reach lldpd.

relevant_fields: hostname: device hostname -- used as LLDP System Name TLV; if missing lldpd may advertise IP or blank
lldp_tx_interval: LLDP transmit interval in seconds (default 30); controls how often LLDPDUs are sent and indirectly controls TTL
lldp_tx_hold: TTL multiplier (default 4); TTL advertised = lldp_tx_interval * lldp_tx_hold
mac: chassis MAC address used as fallback Chassis ID TLV if no other identifier configured
platform: platform identifier included in System Description TLV
description: port description string -- advertised as LLDP Port Description TLV to neighbors
admin_status: up/down -- LLDP only operates on admin-up ports
alias: human-readable port alias -- may be used in Port ID TLV depending on lldpd configuration
mtu: MTU -- included in IEEE 802.3 Maximum Frame Size TLV
speed: interface speed -- included in IEEE 802.3 MAC/PHY Configuration TLV
lldp_rem_chassis_id: neighbor chassis ID (typically neighbor MAC or system ID)
lldp_rem_chassis_id_subtype: chassis ID encoding (1=component, 4=MAC, 5=network address, 7=locally assigned)
lldp_rem_port_id: neighbor port identifier (interface name or MAC)
lldp_rem_port_id_subtype: port ID encoding
lldp_rem_sys_name: neighbor hostname (System Name TLV)
lldp_rem_sys_desc: neighbor system description (OS version, platform)
lldp_rem_port_desc: neighbor port description
lldp_rem_man_addr: neighbor management address
lldp_rem_sys_cap_supported: bitmask of neighbor capabilities
lldp_rem_sys_cap_enabled: bitmask of neighbor enabled capabilities
trap_ids: lldp -- specifies which trap type this policer applies to
trap_action: trap -- forward to CPU; copy -- copy to CPU and forward in hardware
cir: committed information rate in pps -- LLDP frames exceeding this rate are dropped
cbs: committed burst size -- burst allowance above the CIR
meter_type: packets -- rate measured in packets per second for L2 traps
mode: sr_tcm or tr_tcm -- policing mode
gwaddr: management gateway -- used when lldpmgrd constructs the management address TLV
(key contains the IP/prefix): management IP address advertised in LLDP Management Address TLV

sonic_notes.note: LLDP in SONiC runs inside the 'lldp' Docker container. It consists of three processes managed by supervisord: lldpd (the protocol daemon), lldpmgrd (reads CONFIG_DB and configures lldpd), and lldpsyncd (reads lldpd neighbor table and writes to STATE_DB). All three must be running for end-to-end LLDP functionality. Check all three with 'docker exec lldp supervisorctl status' before assuming a single-process failure.

lldpmgrd is the CONFIG_DB-to-lldpd bridge, analogous to bgpcfgd for BGP. It calls lldpcli (lldpd's CLI tool) to configure lldpd at runtime whenever CONFIG_DB changes. Direct lldpcli commands made outside of lldpmgrd may be overwritten on the next lldpmgrd CONFIG_DB sync event. For persistent LLDP configuration, always use 'config lldp ...' SONiC CLI commands or modify CONFIG_DB directly and let lldpmgrd pick up the change.

lldpsyncd uses polling (not event-driven) to read lldpd's neighbor table via 'lldpcli -f json show neighbors'. It polls at a fixed interval (typically 10 seconds) and diffs against the current STATE_DB state. This means STATE_DB LLDP neighbor data can be up to 10 seconds stale relative to what lldpd knows internally. Use 'docker exec lldp lldpcli show neighbors' for the freshest view of LLDP state.

LLDP multicast frames (01:80:C2:00:00:0E) are not forwarded by switches operating as Layer 2 bridges -- this is by IEEE design. If a SONiC switch is acting as a pure L2 switch, LLDP only discovers directly connected neighbors one hop away. It cannot see topology beyond the local link. This is a common source of confusion when operators expect LLDP to behave like CDP in a multi-hop context.

On SONiC switches with LAG (Link Aggregation) ports, LLDP runs on the individual member ports (Ethernet0, Ethernet4, etc.) rather than the LAG interface (PortChannel0). Operators looking for LLDP neighbors on PortChannel interfaces will not find them -- they must check the member ports. 'show lldp table' shows all interfaces including LAG members.
sonic_notes.source_ref: SONiC LLDP design -- lldpd interface binding

sonic_notes.note: The CoPP rate limit for LLDP defaults are designed for typical access-layer port counts. On 64-port or 128-port spine/aggregation switches in SONiC 202511, the default CoPP rate may be too low. With 64 ports at a 30-second tx-interval, the expected LLDP frame arrival rate is ~2 pps -- well within defaults. However, if multiple VLANs, multiple neighbors per port, or rapid topology changes are involved, the rate can spike significantly. Monitor CoPP counters proactively on high-density platforms.

In SONiC, the 'show lldp neighbors' and 'show lldp table' commands read from STATE_DB (Redis DB 6) LLDP_ENTRY_TABLE. If lldpsyncd is stopped, these commands will show stale data from the last sync. Always cross-check with 'docker exec lldp lldpcli show neighbors' which reads directly from lldpd in-memory state to determine if the issue is in the protocol layer or in the SONiC sync pipeline.

sonic_frr_mapping.frr_process: N/A -- LLDP is not managed by FRR in SONiC; lldpd is a separate open-source daemon
sonic_frr_mapping.config_managed_by: lldpmgrd (reads CONFIG_DB DEVICE_METADATA and PORT tables, calls lldpcli to configure lldpd)

key_commands: docker exec lldp lldpcli show neighbors
docker exec lldp lldpcli show neighbors details
docker exec lldp lldpcli show statistics
docker exec lldp lldpcli show configuration
